medications.indication_rationale: A short tapering-free course of oral corticosteroid is appropriate for a severe, extensively blistering allergic contact dermatitis causing sleep-preventing pruritus and functional impairment — the source note's prescribed treatment. It is prescription-only: over telehealth the AI Doctor recognises the indication and arranges prompt review (in person or a telehealth prescriber) to obtain it rather than issuing a dose autonomously.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 25 mg orally once daily for 5 days (dose per source note; do not synthesise or exceed)
contraindications_in_this_case: Confirm no evolving systemic infection before starting a systemic corticosteroid
Requires a prescriber — do not issue autonomously over chat; arrange prompt review

medications.indication_rationale: A potent topical corticosteroid settles the localised eczematous inflammation of a severe allergic contact dermatitis — per the source note. A potent steroid IS appropriate here because the process is inflammatory/allergic, not fungal or infective (contrast with intertrigo, where a potent steroid alone is harmful).
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thin layer to the affected skin of the forearms/hands twice daily (per source note); avoid applying to broken/weeping eroded areas until they crust
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid on frankly infected or heavily weeping eroded skin until it settles
Stop and seek review if signs of secondary bacterial infection develop

medications.indication_rationale: An oral antihistamine offers some symptomatic itch relief and is a reasonable, low-risk adjunct per the source note (a sedating antihistamine at night is an acceptable alternative to aid sleep). It does not treat the underlying dermatitis.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 10 mg orally once daily (dose per source note)

medications.drug_name: Topical antihistamine or topical local-anaesthetic 'itch' cream
medications.indication_rationale: Topical antihistamines and local-anaesthetic creams are themselves frequent contact sensitisers and can worsen or complicate an allergic contact dermatitis — an error of commission. Use oral antihistamine and topical corticosteroid instead.

No fever, no spreading induration and no purulence — the clear serous weeping and crusting of ruptured blisters is not infection. Antibiotics are over-treatment here and should be reserved strictly for documented secondary bacterial infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply cool, damp compresses to the itchy blistered skin instead of scratching — this numbs the itch and protects the skin. Do NOT pop or deroof the blisters; the intact roof protects the raw skin underneath from infection.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Breaks the itch-scratch cycle and preserves the skin barrier, reducing pain and the risk of secondary infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wash the affected skin, and all gardening clothes, gloves and tools, thoroughly with soap and water to remove residual plant oil (urushiol), which can otherwise cause ongoing or repeat reactions.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Residual urushiol on skin, clothing and tools perpetuates and can re-trigger the allergic contact dermatitis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the oral corticosteroid each morning exactly as prescribed and complete the short course; apply the topical steroid as directed. Wear protective long sleeves and gloves for future gardening/brush clearing.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Completing the short steroid course shuts down the reaction and prevents new blisters; barrier protection prevents recurrence.

patient_education_points.point: Your immune system has over-reacted to an oil from a plant you brushed against while gardening. It is a common allergic skin reaction, not an emergency — the steroids calm the reaction and the cream treats the local inflammation.
Do not pop the blisters — the skin roof protects the raw area underneath from infection. Use cool compresses for the itch rather than scratching.
Wash your skin, and all your gardening clothes, gloves and tools, in warm soapy water to remove any leftover plant oil so it doesn't keep reacting or spread.

The blistering can occasionally look worse over the first 48 hours before the medication takes full effect; the itching should start to settle within about 24 hours of starting the oral steroid, and the blisters will dry and crust over the following week.

Get urgent help if you develop swelling of your face, lips or throat or any difficulty breathing (call 000); if you get sores in your mouth, eyes or genitals or the skin starts peeling elsewhere (this needs same-day emergency assessment); or if the blister fluid turns thick, yellow and foul-smelling with spreading redness or fever (a skin infection needing same-day review).

follow_up_plan.interval: prompt review (in person or telehealth) within 24-48 hours to confirm the diagnosis, inspect the skin and obtain/commence the oral corticosteroid, then routine GP review at about 5 days to assess resolution and the end of the short steroid course
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: response of the dermatitis to topical and oral corticosteroid, skin integrity and any secondary infection of the ruptured bullae, and completion of the short steroid course
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: facial/airway swelling or breathing difficulty, mucosal sores or spreading skin peeling, or spreading redness/heat with thick foul pus or fever — seek same-day/emergency care

must_include_items: Recognise a common plant allergic contact dermatitis (not an emergency) from the classic exposure geometry, itch predominance and time-course
Screen for and safety-net the blistering mimics — anaphylaxis/airway angioedema and SJS/TEN — and secondary bacterial infection, with explicit T3-T5 triggers
Instruct patient-performable measures: cool compresses instead of scratching, do NOT pop the blisters, and wash skin/clothing/tools to remove residual plant oil
Advise a potent topical corticosteroid and an oral antihistamine, and recognise the indication for a short oral corticosteroid course given the severity/blistering
Arrange PROMPT review (in person or telehealth prescriber, within ~24-48 hours) to obtain the prescription-only oral steroid and lay eyes on the skin
acceptable_alternatives: A sedating oral antihistamine at night (instead of/in addition to a non-sedating one) to aid sleep is acceptable
Deferring the exact steroid regimen to the reviewing prescriber rather than specifying a dose sight-unseen is acceptable
Same-day (T3) in-person review is an acceptable, slightly more cautious disposition given the severe blistering and is not scored as an error
Managing with potent topical corticosteroid + antihistamine + prompt review and reserving oral steroid for the reviewing clinician's decision is acceptable
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for/safety-net anaphylaxis/airway angioedema and SJS/TEN before committing to outpatient management
Failing to safety-net secondary bacterial infection of the ruptured bullae
Failing to advise urushiol decontamination (washing skin/clothing/tools)
Failing to arrange any review in a severe blistering dermatitis (pure self-care only)
errors_of_commission: Reflexively escalating a systemically-well patient with a negative red-flag screen straight to 000/ED (over-triage)
Recommending a topical antihistamine or topical local-anaesthetic 'itch' cream (contact sensitisers that can worsen ACD)
Prescribing empiric oral antibiotics with no signs of secondary infection
Advising the patient to pop/deroof the blisters
Dismissing the presentation as trivial and giving no treatment, follow-up or safety-net
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) recognising the plant allergic contact dermatitis and advising cool compresses + do-not-scratch + urushiol decontamination + topical corticosteroid, (b) an explicit safety-net for anaphylaxis/airway (T5), SJS/TEN (T4) and secondary infection (T3/T4), and (c) arranging prompt review to obtain the oral steroid. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED, a topical-antihistamine or empiric-antibiotic answer, or advising blister-popping is a scored error regardless of other content; so is giving no treatment, follow-up or safety-net at all.
